Learning Objectives

Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson, you should be able to:

  • 01Explain what a DBMS is and what it can do.
  • 02Explain the key components of a relational database.
  • 03Conduct restriction queries using one or more conditions with Boolean logic.
  • 04Describe and apply table joins, including how primary and foreign keys enable relationships (one-to-one, one-to-many/many-to-one).

Spatial + attribute data

GIS data are often split into two components: (1) the coordinate information for an object’s geometry, and (2) the attribute information — the nonspatial properties of that object.

Because attribute information is usually presented to the user in tables, it’s often called tabular data.

The database management system

Attribute information in a GIS is typically entered, analyzed, and reported using a database management system (DBMS) — a specialized program for organizing and manipulating data. A spreadsheet can hold a table too, but a DBMS adds the guardrails that make shared data trustworthy: it enforces data types and keys, lets many people read and write at once without clobbering each other, and recovers cleanly after a crash.

What a DBMS does, in general:

  • ›Stores and retrieves large amounts of data efficiently
  • ›Maintains data integrity (types, constraints, keys) to reduce errors
  • ›Supports reliability and recovery (backup, restore, crash recovery)
  • ›Manages access and security (permissions, roles, auditing)
  • ›Enables multi-user work (concurrent reads/writes)

Anatomy of a database

  • ColColumn (field / attribute / item) — a vertical set of values that all represent the same kind of information. Each column has a name and a data type (number, text, date…).
  • RowRow (record / tuple / instance) — a single horizontal entry containing all the column values for one item (one county, one parcel, one person).
  • CellElement (cell / value) — one value at the intersection of a row and a column (e.g. the FIPS for Clallam County is 53009).
  • TblTable (relation / entity) — a structured collection of data organized into rows and columns.

Because every value in a column shares one data type, the DBMS can validate entries as they’re typed, sort and index them quickly, and run calculations on them reliably.

How it fits into a larger system

In many applications (including ArcGIS), the DBMS is the database tier in a multi-tier architecture — the desktop or browser client and an application server sit on top of it.

Relational databases & keys

Relational databases store data in tables, and tables can be related — so information in one table can be connected to information in another. Splitting data across related tables lets each fact be stored once: update a forest’s size in a single place and every query sees the change, avoiding the contradictory copies that creep into one giant table.

Keys

Tables are related through keys — one or more columns that meet certain requirements and can be used to index the rows. A key is often a column that uniquely identifies every row; we frequently assign a unique number or code as the key (much like a Social Security number identifies a person). Keys are what let us join data in one table to associated data in another.

A good key is stable and never reused, which is why systems often add a dedicated ID column rather than relying on names that can change or repeat. Databases also build an index on key columns — a lookup structure that finds matching rows without scanning the whole table.

Queries & Boolean logic

Queries are among the most common operations in a DBMS: through a query we ask the database to return specific information. Relational databases often support SQL (Structured Query Language, pronounced “sequel” or “ess-cue-el”). Many GIS programs also provide a query builder — a graphical interface for building selections, such as ArcGIS Pro’s Select by Attributes.

The restrict (selection) operation

One key query type selects (or restricts) a subset of records based on the values of specified attributes.

A simple selection uses a single condition; more complex queries chain multiple conditions with Boolean operators.

The common Boolean operators in a selection query are AND, OR, and NOT:

Let’s think
Does an OR condition return more, fewer, or the same number of records as an AND condition?
Answer OR returns more (or the same). A quick intuition: AND is the overlap of two conditions (an intersection), so it can only narrow a result; OR is the combination of them (a union), so it can only broaden it.
Group exercise

Which states meet each condition?

Using the table below, work out which states satisfy each selection. Try each one, then raise your hand and I’ll call on someone to walk us through it.

  • 1Smokers < 20%
  • 2Smokers > 20% AND illiteracy < 10
  • 3NOT (non-federal taxes > 9)
  • 4Illiteracy < 7 OR income > 22,000
  • 5Gets more federal aid than paid in taxes AND non-federal taxes > 9
  • 6[Firearm deaths < 10 AND income > 21,000] AND NOT (smokers > 20)

Table joins & keys

Relational databases are powerful partly because we can structure data to reduce duplication, ease maintenance, and stay flexible — and much of that flexibility comes from being able to join tables.

A join (also called a relate) uses a column in one table to match rows in another: a single column in one table is matched to a column in another, and a new table is displayed by combining the rows with matching values. That joined table is usually a temporary view rather than a new stored table — the link lives in the relationship, and you save it out only if you need a permanent copy.

The two most common types of join differ in what they keep:

  • InnerAn inner join keeps only matching records.
  • OuterAn outer join keeps all records, matched or not (unmatched rows get nulls).

A worked example: what is John Smith’s address?

Student information and address information live in separate tables, linked by StudentID.

A join works through a primary key in the source table and a foreign key in the target table:

  • PKA primary key uniquely identifies each row in the source table and cannot be null.
  • FKA foreign key is a field in the target table used to link back to rows in another table.

For a join to work, the two key fields must be compatible types — you can’t reliably match a number to text.

Schema

A schema is the blueprint of a database: it defines how data is organized — the tables, their columns and data types, and the rules and relationships between tables (primary keys, foreign keys, and constraints).

Relationships between tables

In a GIS you mostly encounter one-to-one and many-to-one (or one-to-many) relationships. One-to-many is by far the most common in practice — one feature tied to many records (one parcel, many sales; one stream gauge, many readings).

One-to-one

Each row in one table matches exactly one row in the other. The employee can manage one department, and the department can be managed by one employee.

Many-to-one

Many rows in one table relate to a single row in the other. Each employee belongs to one department, and each department has many employees.

When both sides can have many matches (a many-to-many relationship), databases usually resolve it with a third “bridge” table that pairs the keys.

Let’s think
Below, the Hunter ID is a primary key in the Hunter table and a foreign key in the Bog Record table. Is this a one-to-one or a one-to-many relationship?
Answer One-to-many. Each hunter appears once in the Hunter table (primary key) but can have many entries in the Bog Record table (the foreign key repeats) — one hunter, many records.
Group exercise

How many rows?

For the two tables below, how many rows would an inner join produce? An outer join? Work it out and raise your hand.

How to reason it out An inner join keeps only rows whose key value appears in both tables — so count the matching keys (and remember a key matching several rows multiplies the pairings). An outer join keeps every row from the tables regardless of a match, filling unmatched fields with nulls, so it has at least as many rows as the inner join.

Key takeaways

1
Most GIS attribute data is tabular and is handled through a DBMS that organizes and manipulates data reliably.
2
Queries (via SQL or a query builder) restrict/select subsets of records using Boolean operators.
3
Relational databases get their power from linking tables via keys and combining them through joins — with different outputs depending on the join type.

Key terms & concepts

Open any term to see its definition and an example.
